sections.content:

You are still on the same level.
The game is over.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 20 of about 33 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 20 actions returned the board to a state already seen 20 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 20 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over and the level has reset. Looking at the adjacency list, I can see the chain structure is more complex than I thought. The white diamond (id=6) is connected to:
- id=2 (B, black background)
- id=7 (O, orange square)
Wait, looking at the adjacency more carefully:
- 6(W) - 7(O): white diamond connected to orange square
- 8(G) - 12(O): gray diamond connected to orange outline pixel
- 14(O) - 17(M): orange square connected to magenta (inside it?)
- 30(G) - 37(O): gray diamond connected to orange outline pixel
- 43(G) - 44(p): gray diamond connected to purple (inside it?)
- 47(p) - 48(M): purple square connected to magenta (inside it?)
- 53(G) - 56(p): gray diamond connected to purple (inside it?)
So the chain structure seems to be:
- Top chain: W(6) - O(14) - G(8) - p(outline at 18,57)
- Bottom chain: G(30) - G(43) - p(47) - G(53) - O(outline at 49,40)
Wait, but the adjacency shows:
- 6(W) - 7(O): white diamond connected to orange square (id 7)
- 14(O) - 17(M): orange square (id 14) connected to magenta
- 8(G) - 12(O): gray diamond connected to orange outline pixel
Hmm, the IDs are confusing. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the adjacency list, the key connections for chain objects are:
- 6(W) - 7(O): white diamond connected to orange square
- 8(G) - 12(O): gray diamond connected to orange outline pixel (or another orange object)
- 14(O) - 17(M): orange square connected to magenta
- 30(G) - 37(O): gray diamond connected to orange outline pixel
- 43(G) - 44(p): gray diamond connected to purple
- 47(p) - 48(M): purple square connected to magenta
- 53(G) - 56(p): gray diamond connected to purple
Wait, I think the chain objects are:
- White diamond: id 6
- Orange square: id 14 (with magenta child 17)
- Gray diamonds: ids 8, 30, 43, 53
- Purple square: id 47 (with magenta child 48)
- Purple outline: connected to id 56 (purple)
- Orange outline: connected to ids 12, 37 (orange)
The chain structure from the image shows:
- Top chain: W → O → G → p (purple outline)
- Bottom chain: G → G → p → G → O (orange outline)
But the adjacency list shows these objects are all connected to the black background (id 2), which means they're not directly connected to each other. The connections are through the background.
Actually, looking at the image, the chains are connected by white lines. The white lines are part of the black background or a separate object.
Let me re-examine the segmentation to understand the chain structure better.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the approach. The level has reset, and I need to start fresh with a better understanding of the mechanics.
From my previous attempts:
1. Clicking on the orange square causes the white diamond to disappear and the purple square to move towards the orange square.
2. Clicking on chain objects moves the white diamond along the chain.
3. Clicking on the purple square when the white diamond is not in the chain causes a game over.
The goal seems to be to get the white diamond to the orange outline at (49, 40). But how?
Looking at the Level 1 solution:
1. Click on the purple outline → white diamond moves to outline
2. Click on the tail (gray diamond) → chain reverses
3. Click on an outline pixel → level completes
For Level 2, maybe the solution is similar but with different objects. Let me try:
1. Click on the orange outline at (49, 40) → white diamond moves to outline
2. Click on the tail (gray diamond or orange square) → chain reverses
3. Click on an outline pixel → level completes
But wait, in my previous attempts, clicking on the orange outline caused the white diamond to move to the outline. Then clicking on the orange square caused the white diamond to disappear. Then clicking on the purple square caused a game over.
Maybe the issue is that I clicked on the wrong object after the white diamond moved to the outline. In Level 1, after clicking the outline, I clicked on the tail (gray diamond) to reverse the chain. In Level 2, maybe I need to click on a different object.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of clicking on the orange square, let me click on the gray diamond at the end of the chain (opposite the white diamond) after the white diamond moves to the outline.
But first, I need to understand the chain structure in the initial state. Looking at the image:
- Top chain: W (4, 17) → O (10, 23) → G (7, 49) → p (purple outline at 18, 57)
- Bottom chain: G (19, 8) → G (33, 45) → p (39, 48) → G (46, 54) → O (orange outline at 49, 40)
Wait, but the white diamond is at (4, 17), which is at the top of the top chain. The orange square is at (10, 23), which is connected to the white diamond. The gray diamond is at (7, 49), which is connected to the orange square. The purple outline is at (18, 57), which is connected to the gray diamond.
So the top chain is: W → O → G → p (outline)
And the bottom chain is: G → G → p → G → O (outline)
But the white diamond is only on the top chain. How does it get to the orange outline on the bottom chain?
Maybe I need to click on the purple square to transfer the white diamond to the bottom chain?
Let me try clicking on the purple square at (39, 48) to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Level has reset. Let me understand the chain structure better.
# Looking at the image:
# Top chain: W(4,17) → O(10,23) → G(7,49) → p(outline at 18,57)
# Bottom chain: G(19,8) → G(33,45) → p(39,48) → G(46,54) → O(outline at 49,40)
# 
# The white diamond is on the top chain. The orange outline is on the bottom chain.
# I need to get the white diamond to the orange outline.
#
# In Level 1, the solution was:
# 1. Click on purple outline → white diamond moves to outline
# 2. Click on tail (gray diamond) → chain reverses
# 3. Click on outline pixel → level completes
#
# For Level 2, maybe I need to:
# 1. Click on purple square to transfer white diamond to bottom chain
# 2. Click on chain objects to move white diamond to orange outline
# 3. Click on outline pixel to complete level
#
# Let me try clicking on the purple square at (39, 48)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 49}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
